title: Upper Ring Dawn
desc: Before the upper terraces fill, the sunrise crystals and hanging light veils need their morning check.

steps.text:
The upper ring of Kiru City belongs to the early hour before it belongs to anyone else. Couriers have not started running in full numbers yet. The inner gates are only beginning to unbar. Below you, the concentric streets of the city hold their shape in cool blue shadow while the first light creeps over the outer walls. Along the overlook rail, crystal housings and pale woven veils wait to catch that light and turn it into something softer than glare and brighter than torchfire.

The terrace keeper has already unlocked the maintenance chest. "Take the bridge line and check the wake-up crystals if you want the finer work," she says. "Or retie the light veils along the overlook if you would rather deal with cloth and wind. Either way, the city will notice if dawn comes through here badly."

options.label: Walk the bridge line and inspect the morning crystals
Retie the hanging veils along the overlook rail

steps.text:
The crystal housings collect exactly what a high terrace promises them: mist from the falls, dust from the city below, and the thin grit that travels farther on morning wind than it has any right to. You unfasten each cover, wipe the inner facets, and turn two of the mounts by fractions until the first edge of sunlight enters cleanly instead of scattering wild. One by one the crystals wake. Gold first, then a clearer white where the angle is best, then long bands of light laid across the bridge stones like something measured rather than accidental.

By the time you reach the last post, Kiru City has started answering the sun. Lower windows catch. Upper banners stir. The keeper looks down across the rings and gives a satisfied nod that feels almost ceremonial. "There," she says. "Now the city looks like it remembers what it is."

options.label: Latch the housings and leave the terrace in first light

steps.text:
The veils are woven to soften glare without stealing the view, which means they behave beautifully right up until a hard night wind twists them into sulks. You work the overlook rail from one end to the other, loosening snarled ties, replacing two frayed silk loops, and drawing each panel back into even tension so the morning air can lift them instead of fighting them. When they are set correctly, they do not hang so much as hover, rising and settling in measured breaths above the drop.

As the sun clears the walls, the veils catch the light and spread it across the terrace in pale moving bands. From here the city's rings, towers, courtyards, and roof tiles all seem to brighten in sequence. The keeper braces a hand on the rail beside you and watches the change travel outward. "People below will call it a beautiful morning," she says. "That is how you know the work disappeared properly."

options.label: Coil the spare silk and leave the veils riding the dawn
